title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Chocolate has a rich history that dates (0) back thousands of years to the ancient civilizations of Mesoamerica, where it was highly prized. (9) Although today we consume it mostly as a sweet treat, for the Mayans and Aztecs, it was primarily a bitter beverage. (10) Such was the value of cacao beans that they were even used as a form of currency in trade. (11) Provided that a person was of high social status, they could enjoy this 'drink of the gods' during sacred rituals. (12) Not only was cacao important for its taste, but it was also believed to possess significant medicinal properties. (13) Regardless of the long journey across the Atlantic, chocolate quickly became a luxury item in the royal courts of Europe. (14) Not until the Industrial Revolution did the mass production of solid chocolate bars become possible for the wider population. (15) In spite of its global popularity, the farming of cacao remains a difficult and labor-intensive process in tropical regions. (16) Whether the future of chocolate is threatened by climate change is a major concern for scientists and manufacturers alike. This beloved substance continues to be a symbol of indulgence and comfort across many different cultures.

example.text: Chocolate has a rich history that dates (0) back thousands of years.
example.explanation: Adverb used to indicate a point in the past.

questions.explanation: Conjunction used to introduce a contrast.
Determiner used for emphasis before a noun phrase.
Conjunction meaning 'if' or 'on the condition that'.
Part of the 'Not only... but also' structure.
Part of the fixed phrase 'Regardless of'.
Part of the 'Not until' structure triggering inversion ('did the mass production... become').
Part of the prepositional linker 'In spite of'.
Conjunction introducing a doubt between options.
